section.title: Laws of Offerings and the Sabbath-Breaker
section.sectionRationale: Right after the crushing judgment of chapter 14, God turns immediately to laws that presume Israel will one day actually enter the land, distinguishes unintentional sin from defiant rebellion, narrates one stark case of deliberate Sabbath-breaking, and closes with the command to wear tassels as a perpetual reminder of obedience, together reassuring a devastated generation that God's covenant purpose for their children still stands.
section.thesis: In the immediate aftermath of judgment, God gives laws that assume Israel will still enter Canaan, provides atonement for unintentional sin while holding defiant sin accountable, judges one man's open, deliberate Sabbath-breaking, and commands a visible reminder, tassels on every garment, so that the next generation will remember and obey rather than repeat their parents' unbelief.
section.frameNote: Five movements: offerings for when Israel enters the land, presuming a future beyond judgment; the offering of firstfruits from the dough; the distinction between unintentional sin, which atonement covers, and defiant sin, which cuts a person off; the case of the man who deliberately gathered wood on the Sabbath; and the command to wear tassels as a lasting reminder of God's commandments.

units.label: Offerings when you enter the land
units.note: God speaks of a future beyond judgment

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and tell them: After you enter the land that I am giving you as a home

and you present an offering made by fire to the LORD from the herd or flock to produce a pleasing aroma to the LORD, either a burnt offering or a sacrifice, for a special vow or freewill offering or appointed feast,
then the one presenting his offering to the LORD shall also present a grain offering of a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a quarter hin of olive oil.
With the burnt offering or sacrifice of each lamb, you are to prepare a quarter hin of wine as a drink offering.
With a ram you are to prepare a grain offering of two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a third of a hin of olive oil,
and a third of a hin of wine as a drink offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
When you prepare a young bull as a burnt offering or sacrifice to fulfill a vow or as a peace offering to the LORD,
present with the bull a grain offering of three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with half a hin of olive oil.
Also present half a hin of wine as a drink offering. It is an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
This is to be done for each bull, ram, lamb, or goat.
This is how you must prepare each one, no matter how many.
Everyone who is native-born shall prepare these things in this way when he presents an offering made by fire as a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
And for the generations to come, if a foreigner residing with you or someone else among you wants to prepare an offering made by fire as a pleasing aroma to the LORD, he is to do exactly as you do.
The assembly is to have the same statute both for you and for the foreign resident; it is a permanent statute for the generations to come. You and the foreigner shall be the same before the LORD.
The same law and the same ordinance will apply both to you and to the foreigner residing with you.”
Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and tell them: When you enter the land to which I am bringing you
and you eat the food of the land, you shall lift up an offering to the LORD.
From the first of your dough, you are to lift up a cake as a contribution; offer it just like an offering from the threshing floor.
Throughout your generations, you are to give the LORD an offering from the first of your dough.

units.label: Unintentional sin and defiant sin
units.note: atonement covers one, not the other

verses.text: Now if you stray {{unintentional|unintentionally}} and do not obey all these commandments that the LORD has spoken to Moses,
all that the LORD has commanded you through Moses from the day the LORD gave them and continuing through the generations to come,

and if it was done unintentionally without the knowledge of the congregation, then the whole congregation is to prepare one young bull as a burnt offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD, with its grain offering and drink offering according to the regulation, and one male goat as a sin offering.

The priest is to make atonement for the whole congregation of Israel, so that they may be forgiven; for the sin was unintentional and they have brought to the LORD an offering made by fire and a sin offering, presented before the LORD for their unintentional sin.
Then the whole congregation of Israel and the foreigners residing among them will be forgiven, since it happened to all the people unintentionally.
Also, if one person sins unintentionally, he is to present a year-old female goat as a sin offering.
And the priest shall make atonement before the LORD on behalf of the person who erred by sinning unintentionally; and when atonement has been made for him, he will be forgiven.
You shall have the same law for the one who acts in error, whether he is a native-born Israelite or a foreigner residing among you.
But the person who sins defiantly, whether a native or foreigner, blasphemes the LORD. That person shall be cut off from among his people.
He shall certainly be cut off, because he has despised the word of the LORD and broken His commandment; his guilt remains on him.”
While the Israelites were in the wilderness, a man was found gathering wood on the Sabbath day.
Those who found the man gathering wood brought him to Moses, Aaron, and the whole congregation,
and because it had not been declared what should be done to him, they placed him in custody.
And the LORD said to Moses, “The man must surely be put to death. The whole congregation is to stone him outside the camp.”
So the whole congregation took the man outside the camp and stoned him to death, as the LORD had commanded Moses.
Later, the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and tell them that throughout the generations to come they are to make for themselves {{tassels|tassels}} for the corners of their garments, with a blue cord on each tassel.

These will serve as tassels for you to look at, so that you may remember all the commandments of the LORD, that you may obey them and not prostitute yourselves by following your own heart and your own eyes.
Then you will remember and obey all My commandments, and you will be holy to your God.
I am the LORD your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God. I am the LORD your God.”

tassels.gloss: Tassels or fringes attached to the corners of a garment, with a blue cord on each. Meant to be looked at and to serve as a constant, visible reminder to obey God's commandments rather than follow one's own heart and eyes.

unintentional.gloss: Unintentional sin or error, done without full knowledge or deliberate intent. The law distinguishes this, which atonement can cover, from sin done beyad ramah, with a high hand, which is defiant and knowing and cannot be remedied by the same ritual means.

basic.context: Right after announcing that the adult generation will die in the wilderness, God gives Moses laws that presume Israel will still, one day, actually enter the land: offerings to be made there, a portion of dough set aside as firstfruits, and a clear distinction between unintentional sin, which atonement covers, and defiant, high-handed sin, which cuts a person off from the people. A specific case of deliberate Sabbath-breaking follows, and the chapter closes with the command to wear tassels on every garment as a perpetual visual reminder to obey.
basic.hermeneutics: This is legal instruction embedded within narrative, remarkable for its timing: immediately after judgment, God speaks as though the covenant future is not cancelled but simply deferred to the next generation. Its case law about the Sabbath-breaker illustrates the earlier distinction between unintentional and defiant sin with a concrete, sobering example, while the tassels close the chapter with a positive, forward-looking provision for remembering obedience.
basic.originalLanguages: The law distinguishes sin done shegagah, in error or unintentionally, from sin done with a high hand, defiantly; the first can be atoned for through prescribed offerings, but the second, a direct, contemptuous rejection of God's word, cannot be covered by ritual and results in being cut off. The tassels commanded at the chapter's end function as a wearable, ever-present reminder not to follow one's own heart and eyes into that same defiance.
basic.typology: The distinction between atoned unintentional sin and unatoned defiant sin anticipates the New Testament's serious warnings against willful, ongoing sin after receiving the knowledge of the truth, Hebrews 10:26-27, while the tassels, worn to remember God's commandments, anticipate the greater remembrance believers now have through the Spirit writing God's law on the heart, Jeremiah 31:33, Hebrews 8:10.
basic.christ: Where the law here provides atonement only for unintentional sin and leaves defiant sin without ritual remedy, Christ's atonement is sufficient for all who come to Him in genuine repentance, even from the most willful rebellion, since His blood cleanses from all sin, 1 John 1:7, provided it is not the final, hardened rejection this passage's high-handed sin pictures.
basic.god: God is both gracious and just in the same breath: gracious enough to provide continued instruction for a future He knows this generation will not see, and just enough to require real consequences for defiant, contemptuous sin, shown starkly in the Sabbath-breaker's fate. His command for tassels reveals a God who wants His people to remember Him not occasionally but continually, woven into the fabric of daily life.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 10:26-27 warns of no further sacrifice for sin for those who deliberately keep on sinning after receiving the knowledge of the truth, echoing this chapter's distinction. 1 John 1:7 declares the blood of Jesus cleanses from all sin. Matthew 9:20-22 records a woman touching the fringe, likely these very tassels, of Jesus' garment for healing. Jeremiah 31:33 promises God's law written on the heart.
basic.oneStory: Even in the immediate aftermath of judgment, God continues to provide for His people's future, distinguishing between sin that can be atoned for and sin that hardens into defiance, and giving a visible reminder to keep His commandments. That pattern of provision, distinction, and remembrance finds its fulfillment in Christ, whose blood cleanses all who come to Him and whose Spirit writes God's law on the heart rather than merely on a tassel.

context.literary: This chapter's placement immediately after the devastating judgment of chapter 14 is deliberate: God's giving of laws presuming a future in the land signals that His covenant purposes continue for Israel's children even as that generation's own hope of entering Canaan has ended.
context.historical: The case of the Sabbath-breaker, verses 32 to 36, is one of only a handful of specific legal cases narrated in full in the Pentateuch, likely included because it needed a direct ruling from the LORD, since the law had not yet specified the exact penalty for this violation.
context.canonical: The command to wear tassels becomes a permanent feature of later Jewish practice, still observed today, and the Gospels record people reaching for the fringe of Jesus' garment, likely these very tassels, in hope of healing, Matthew 9:20-22.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reassure Israel's next generation of God's continuing covenant purpose despite the preceding judgment, to distinguish clearly between forgivable unintentional sin and unforgivable defiant sin, and to provide a lasting, visible reminder to keep God's commandments.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific offerings, the Sabbath-breaker's case, and the tassels describe particular instructions and events for ancient Israel; they do not prescribe identical rituals for believers today, who remember God's commandments through the indwelling Spirit rather than a garment's tassel. The abiding principle is the seriousness of the distinction between unintentional sin and defiant, unrepentant rebellion.

debates.question: Why was the Sabbath-breaker's exact penalty unknown until the LORD specifically revealed it, verses 34 to 35, when the death penalty for Sabbath-breaking had already been stated in Exodus 31:14-15?

views.label: The general law existed, but its specific application needed confirmation
views.case: Exodus had already established death as the penalty in principle, but Israel may have needed the LORD's direct word to confirm exactly how, by stoning, and that the principle truly applied in this concrete case.

views.label: This case served as a formative, sobering lesson for the newly re-instructed generation
views.case: Coming right after the distinction between unintentional and defiant sin, this real case functioned as a vivid, unmistakable illustration of exactly what a high hand looks like in practice.

debates.bearing: Both readings underline that this case study exists to make concrete and unmistakable the difference the chapter has just described in the abstract, between sin that can be atoned for and sin that is open, deliberate defiance.

study.originalLanguages: The law distinguishes shegagah, unintentional sin or error, from sin done with a high hand, defiantly and knowingly; only the former can be atoned for through the prescribed offerings, showing the law itself recognized a category of sin ritual sacrifice was never meant to cover. The tassels commanded at the chapter's close were meant to be looked at, a physical, visual trigger to remember and obey all God's commandments.

typology.type: Unintentional sin covered by atonement, defiant sin left unremedied by ritual
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament's warning against deliberate, ongoing sin after receiving the knowledge of the truth, for which no further sacrifice remains, Hebrews 10:26-27.

typology.type: Tassels worn as a visible reminder of God's commandments
typology.fulfillment: The law written on the heart by the Spirit, a deeper and more permanent remembrance than any external mark, Jeremiah 31:33, Hebrews 8:10.

study.christ: Where the law here provides atonement only for unintentional sin and leaves defiant sin without ritual remedy, Christ's atonement is sufficient for all who come to Him in genuine repentance, even from the most willful rebellion, since His blood cleanses from all sin, 1 John 1:7, provided it is not the final, hardened rejection this passage's high-handed sin pictures.
study.god: God is both gracious and just in the same breath: gracious enough to provide continued instruction for a future He knows this generation will not see, and just enough to require real consequences for defiant, contemptuous sin, shown starkly in the Sabbath-breaker's fate. His command for tassels reveals a God who wants His people to remember Him not occasionally but continually, woven into the fabric of daily life.

crossReferences.note: Warns of no further sacrifice for sin for those who deliberately keep on sinning after receiving the knowledge of the truth, echoing this chapter's distinction.
Declares that the blood of Jesus cleanses from all sin, the fuller answer to this chapter's limited provision for unintentional sin only.
Records a woman touching the fringe, likely the tassels commanded here, of Jesus' garment, believing it would bring healing.
Promises a new covenant in which God's law is written on the heart, surpassing an external, wearable reminder.

study.oneStory: Even in the immediate aftermath of judgment, God continues to provide for His people's future, distinguishing between sin that can be atoned for and sin that hardens into defiance, and giving a visible reminder to keep His commandments. That pattern of provision, distinction, and remembrance finds its fulfillment in Christ, whose blood cleanses all who come to Him and whose Spirit writes God's law on the heart rather than merely on a tassel.

quiz.q: What is notable about the timing of the offering laws given at the start of this chapter?

options.text: They presume Israel will still enter the land, given immediately after the judgment that the adult generation would die in the wilderness.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2 introduce these laws right after chapter 14's judgment, presuming a future in Canaan.

options.text: They were given decades later, after Israel had already entered Canaan.
options.note: This contradicts the chapter's immediate placement right after chapter 14's judgment.

options.text: They replaced all previous offering laws entirely.
options.note: This is not stated or implied anywhere in the text.

options.text: No particular significance to the timing is noted in the text.
options.note: The placement itself carries clear significance given the preceding judgment.

quiz.q: What is the difference in outcome between unintentional sin and defiant, high-handed sin according to this chapter?

options.text: Unintentional sin can be atoned for through prescribed offerings, but a person who sins defiantly is to be cut off from the people.
options.note: Verses 27 to 31 state this distinction exactly.

options.text: Both types of sin receive identical treatment under the law.
options.note: This contradicts the explicit distinction made in verses 27 to 31.

options.text: Defiant sin is treated more leniently since it shows confidence rather than weakness.
options.note: This contradicts the stated consequence of being cut off for defiant sin.

options.text: No distinction between the two types of sin is made.
options.note: Verses 27 to 31 make this distinction directly.

quiz.q: What happened to the man found gathering wood on the Sabbath day?

options.text: He was put in custody, and the LORD commanded that the whole congregation stone him to death outside the camp.
options.note: Verses 32 to 36 describe this exact outcome.

options.text: He was simply warned and released without punishment.
options.note: This contradicts the explicit death sentence carried out in verses 35 to 36.

options.text: He was exiled from the camp for seven days, like Miriam in an earlier chapter.
options.note: That penalty belongs to a different chapter and a different situation, Numbers 12.

options.text: No account of this man's fate is given.
options.note: Verses 32 to 36 record his fate in full.

quiz.q: What were the Israelites commanded to attach to the corners of their garments?

options.text: Tassels, with a blue cord on each tassel.
options.note: Verse 38 states this instruction exactly.

options.text: Small bells, to be heard when they walked.
options.note: This is not commanded in this chapter.

options.text: Written scrolls of the law sewn into their clothing.
options.note: This is not the instruction given here.

options.text: No such instruction is given in this chapter.
options.note: Verse 38 gives this instruction directly.

quiz.q: What was the stated purpose of the tassels, according to verse 39?

options.text: To serve as a reminder to look at and obey all the commandments of the LORD, rather than following one's own heart and eyes.
options.note: Verse 39 states this purpose exactly.

options.text: To mark a person's tribe visually for others to identify.
options.note: This is not the purpose given in the text.

options.text: To indicate wealth and social status among the Israelites.
options.note: This is not the purpose stated in verse 39.

options.text: No purpose for the tassels is given.
options.note: Verse 39 states the purpose directly.

questions: God gave laws presuming a future in the land immediately after judging the very generation that would not see it, showing His covenant purposes continue beyond any one generation's failure. Where do you need to trust that God's larger purposes continue even through a season of personal or communal discipline?

The law distinguished sin done in genuine error from sin done with a defiant, high hand. Is there an area where you have moved from an honest mistake into a settled, knowing pattern of disregard for what you know God has said?

The Sabbath-breaker's case was severe and public, a sobering illustration of what defiant sin actually looks like in practice. How seriously do you take the difference between struggling against sin and simply deciding to disregard God's word?

The tassels existed so Israel would have a constant, physical reminder not to follow their own heart and eyes into unfaithfulness. What practice or reminder in your own life helps you remember God's commands in the middle of ordinary, easily distracted days?

Believers now have God's law written on the heart by the Spirit rather than merely a tassel on a garment. How does that deeper, internal reminder change the way you pursue obedience compared to relying only on external prompts?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew shegagah, unintentional sin or error, stands in deliberate contrast to sin done with a high hand, a vivid idiom for open, deliberate defiance rather than accidental wrongdoing.

This is one of the few instances in the Pentateuch where a specific legal case required a direct, individual ruling from the LORD, since the general law had not yet specified the exact penalty for this situation.
The blue cord attached to each tassel likely used a costly dye associated elsewhere with royalty and the sanctuary, giving even an ordinary garment's corner a marker of consecration to the LORD.
